focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

The noon heat sat under Halima's tin roof like another body, large and breathless, turning every paper damp at the edges. She had spread Rahima Bewa's scraps of proof on the woven mat farthest from the cooking place: a widow certificate with the seal half eaten by mildew, a ration card folded so many times the corners had gone white, and a land-tax receipt yellow as old fat. Rahima herself crouched by the doorway with her smoke-dark shawl slipped off one shoulder, keeping a square powdered-milk tin upright between her ankles. She sold puffed rice from that tin at the ghat and polished the lid with ash every Friday, though the metal had long ago given up shining. Today she had brought it half full and laid two guavas beside Halima's knee, both of them worm-marked near the stem. She never came empty-handed, not even when hunger had made the bones in her wrists look separate.

Nura sat at the window with her good pen and a strip of old newspaper under her hand to keep the ink from taking on damp. She was copying names cleanly for whoever came because half the char could not write the same father twice. Outside, somebody was beating a wet lungi against a bamboo post. The river smell pushed in with the light: silt, fish oil, the soft rot of waterlogged jute. Every few minutes the tin roof gave a dry clicking sound as the sun shifted on it.

Halima squinted down at Rahima's receipt through her clouded eye and the good one both. The number was still legible if she tilted the page away from the glare. "This can stand if the rest of your line stands," she said. "Who can speak for the hut besides you?"

Rahima rubbed a cracked heel against her opposite ankle, scraping off a curl of white skin onto the threshold. "Who speaks for widows?" she said. "The goat, maybe. Th...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...aying you have a book that's truer than the government."

"They say many stupid things before evening prayer."

"They're not saying it like a joke."

No. They were not.

From the road toward the ghat drifted the day's final proof. A boy ran past carrying tea glasses in a wire rack and shouted to another, "Forget the laptop. Ask Halima-dai. She's the real office." By the time his sandals slapped out of hearing, two girls at the tube well had already taken up the phrase and were whispering it into their hands.

Halima stood in the school doorway and looked back once at the improvised table. Chalk dust had settled on the wood beside Aminul's neat stacks of forms. On the blackboard someone had written columns in a schoolboy hand: `Name`, `Father/Husband`, `Household`, `Remarks`. Beneath them, in smaller crooked chalk, someone had added: `Ask Halima`. Nura rubbed at it with her palm until the words blurred.

When she finally started home, the bag at her feet had become heavier than it had been in the morning though she had not taken the bundle out. At the first bend a woman from the north lane slipped out from behind a jute screen with a ration booklet tucked under her blouse and asked whether Halima could come after dark, when her brothers were asleep. Halfway down the path a man from Mofiz's tea circle called from his verandah, smiling too much, "Remember carefully, khalamma. Too much memory can drown a house." Near the western lane, Rahima stepped toward her with a folded paper in hand, saw Nura beside her, and tucked the paper back under her smoke-dark shawl before turning away.

By the time Halima reached her own threshold, three children were squatting across from the yard pretending to play knucklebones and watching to see whether her lamp would stay lit after supper.
